ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general biryani food product class; no specific lamb shank biryani entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a lamb shank biryani specific code. A0FKP (rice-based mixed dish) used as closest parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for lamb shank biryani as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of lamb shank biryani is approximately 450 g, comprising a full lamb shank portion (approximately 200–220 g bone-in meat) served on a bed of spiced basmati rice (approximately 200–230 g cooked rice) with accompanying gravy and garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Biryani (lamb) — nutrient composition estimated from USDA FoodData Central lamb shank (braised) and cooked basmati rice with spices, cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 for lamb biryani analogues
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) and ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for lamb shank biryani exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy lamb shank braised (NDB 17174) at approximately 45% weight contribution and cooked basmati rice with ghee and spices at approximately 50% weight contribution, with ICMR-NIN lamb biryani (per 100 g edible portion) used as a cross-check anchor. The composite per-100 g values reflect a typical restaurant-style lamb shank biryani as served in Singapore and Malaysia.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — basmati rice and mixed rice dishes as analogous estimate basis
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for lamb shank biryani. GI of 58 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for basmati rice-based mixed dishes and biryani analogues (GI range 50–65 for basmati-based biryanis). The fat and protein content from lamb shank moderates the glycemic response relative to plain basmati rice (GI ~58). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (58 × 87.75 g carbohydrate in 450 g serving at 19.5 g/100 g) / 100 ≈ 20.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 310 mg per 100 g; a full 450 g serving delivers approximately 1395 mg sodium, representing 58–70% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit lamb shank biryani to a half-portion or less per meal and avoid adding extra salt or high-sodium condiments such as raita made with salted yoghurt, as the cumulative sodium load from a full serving approaches the upper daily threshold.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index of 58 and a glycemic load of approximately 20 per standard serving; the basmati rice base contributes significant digestible carbohydrate (approximately 88 g per 450 g serving) that will raise postprandial blood glucose.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume lamb shank biryani in a reduced portion (approximately 200–250 g) to keep the glycemic load below 10, pair it with a non-starchy vegetable side, and monitor postprandial glucose, as the medium GI and high carbohydrate load from basmati rice can cause a meaningful rise in blood sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content of approximately 3.1 g per 100 g (approximately 14 g per full serving) from lamb and ghee, combined with elevated sodium, presents a dual cardiovascular risk factor.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit lamb shank biryani to occasional consumption, request reduced ghee preparation where possible, and balance the meal with high-fibre vegetables to partially offset the saturated fat and sodium burden on lipid profiles and blood pressure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lamb Shank Biryani is a rich, aromatic South Asian rice dish that has become a popular restaurant offering across Malaysia and Singapore, particularly in Indian-Muslim (Mamak) and banana-leaf cuisine establishments. The dish features a whole slow-braised or dum-cooked lamb shank — the lower leg portion of the animal — which is marinated in a blend of yoghurt, ginger, garlic, and whole spices including cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, and star anise, then cooked until the meat is fall-off-the-bone tender. The lamb shank is served atop or alongside long-grain basmati rice that has been layered and steamed with saffron or food colouring, fried onions, mint leaves, and ghee using the traditional dum (sealed-pot) method. The result is a deeply flavoured, mildly spiced rice dish with a complex aroma profile combining caramelised onion, warm spice, and rendered lamb fat. The texture contrasts tender, gelatinous lamb meat with fluffy, slightly sticky spiced rice. Nutritionally, a standard 450 g restaurant serving provides approximately 833 kcal, 46 g protein, 35 g fat (including approximately 14 g saturated fat from lamb and ghee), and 88 g carbohydrate. The dish is a significant source of iron, zinc, vitamin B12, and phosphorus from the lamb. The sodium content is high at approximately 1395 mg per serving, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The medium glycemic index (approximately 58) and high glycemic load per serving (approximately 20) require attention from individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is halal and gluten-free but contains dairy (ghee and yoghurt). In Singapore, it is commonly served at banana-leaf restaurants and Indian-Muslim eateries, often accompanied by raita, papadum, and curry gravy.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for lamb shank biryani was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy lamb shank (braised) and cooked basmati rice with ghee and spices, cross-checked against ICMR-NIN lamb biryani analogues. GI is estimated from University of Sydney GI Database basmati rice-based mixed dish analogues. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is systematic and cross-referenced but not based on a direct laboratory analysis of this specific dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are per 100 g of the composite dish as served (lamb shank meat, rice, gravy, and garnishes combined). 2. Serving size of 450 g is an estimate for a standard restaurant plate; actual restaurant portions may vary from 380 g to 550 g. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy or ICMR-NIN sources used for this estimation. 4. selenium_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN lamb biryani analogue source. 5. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate based on basmati rice-based mixed dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for lamb shank biryani was located in peer-reviewed literature. 6. The food_name canonical form uses 'Biryani' (standard international spelling); the input used 'Briyani' (common Malaysian/Singaporean variant), which is preserved in aliases. 7. Halal status is assumed based on the restaurant context (Indian-Muslim banana-leaf establishment); individual restaurant certification should be verified. 8. The cuisine_tags in the input (Noodle, Fried Rice, Roti Prata) appear to be restaurant-level tags for the source establishment rather than dish-specific tags and have not been carried forward as food classification attributes.
